lede: Vessantara / Mahāchat accounts for 85 catalogued manuscripts, 3 of them flagged research-priority. It clusters in Lampang (49% of the corpus for this subject), ahead of Phrae and Nan. Nearly all (99%) are written in Tham Lanna · อักษรธรรมล้านนา script. By support it leans to palm-leaf · ใบลาน (94%) over mulberry paper (saa) · กระดาษสา (6%). Dated witnesses run 1646–1951 CE (69 of 85 carry a date).

authored.title: Vessantara / Mahāchat
authored.body_html:
What this is

The Vessantara — the Mahāchat, "the Great Birth" — is the last and greatest of the jātaka: the story of the prince whose perfection of giving is so total that he gives away his children and his wife. In Lanna it is not merely a text but the text of communal Buddhism, recited in full at the Thet Mahachat festival, and the catalogue holds 85 witnesses whose titles name it. What makes it worth an article of its own is that a single story appears here in four different guises at once — and each guise belongs to a different genre.

One story, four registers

The genre spread tells the story: 76 of the 85 sit under jātaka, but the remainder scatter into Buddhist canonical (4), magic & ritual (3), poetry and liturgy — and the titles make the four modes plain:

  • The thirteen chapters (kan) as scripture — the classic sequence Himaphan, Tanakhan, Wannapawet, Cucok, Cunlapon, Mahapon, Sakkaban, Mahalat, Nakhon — the birth-story proper, copied to be read.
  • The invitation to preach — Kam alathana tham wetsantala, the ariradhana summons that calls a monk to recite the Vessantara at the festival. Here the text is a liturgical cue, tying this entity straight to the chanting corpus.
  • The blessing of hearing it — Anisong wetsantala, the ānisaṃsa that enumerates the merit earned by listening to the whole recitation in one day.
  • The verse retelling for pleasure — Kam len khao wetsantala, the khao that turns scripture into sung entertainment.

The same title is a birth-story on one shelf, a recitation cue on another, a merit-manual on a third and a folk-song on a fourth. Vessantara is the clearest worked example in the collection of how a single work refuses to stay inside one genre.

The shape of the collection

Physically it is canonical — 94% palm-leaf (80 of 85), 99% Tham Lanna — and old, with dated witnesses running 1646–1951 and 69 of the 85 carrying a date. But its geography breaks the usual scholastic pattern: it does not cluster at Wat Sung Men the way plain jātaka does. Lampang leads (42), ahead of Phrae (25) — the Vessantara was copied wherever the Thet Mahachat was performed, which is to say nearly everywhere, rather than concentrated in one great library.

Notes

  • The festival is the through-line. Read this entity beside the liturgy article's ariradhana material: the Kam alathana invitation is the hinge that joins the birth-story to the performed rite. It also frequently binds to katha and anisong in the same volume — Katha wetsantala, Anisong wetsantala — the practical apparatus of putting the recitation on.
  • A candidate for chapter-level indexing. Because the thirteen kan have stable names, the Vessantara is unusually tractable for a finer pass: the Cucok or Mahaphon chapter could each be traced across witnesses. That is the natural next depth for this entity.
  • Not itself research-priority, but a genre bridge. Only 3 of 85 are flagged; its value to the project is structural — it is the single best demonstration that the genre walls in this catalogue are porous, and that performance, not subject, is what moves a text between them.
